gap_analysis.description: Unity Catalog uses an additive (allowlist) permission model with no DENY mechanism.
gap_analysis.remediation: Restructure into separate schemas with restricted grants. Use row filters for partial access. Remove principals from over-privileged groups.

gap_analysis.description: Kerberos principals and service accounts need to be mapped to Databricks service principals or cloud managed identities.
gap_analysis.remediation: Create Databricks service principals for each service account. Configure OAuth M2M authentication. Sync user principals via SCIM from your IdP.

gap_analysis.description: Ranger delegateAdmin allows principals to manage policies for their resources. Unity Catalog uses MANAGE privilege instead.
gap_analysis.remediation: Grant MANAGE privilege on the corresponding UC object, or assign the principal as a metastore/catalog admin if broader admin access is needed.

gap_analysis.description: Ranger wildcard table policies (database.*) translate to schema-level grants in UC. Verify this is intentional.
gap_analysis.remediation: Review if schema-level grants are appropriate. Consider granting on specific tables for least-privilege access.

warnings.message: DENY policy for external_vendors on sales.* [SELECT] — Unity Catalog has no DENY mechanism
warnings.recommendation: Restructure: move sensitive data into a separate schema with restricted grants, or use row filters/column masks to limit visibility instead of denying access.

warnings.recommendation: Restructure: move sensitive data into a separate schema with restricted grants, or use row filters/column masks to limit visibility instead of denying access.

kerberosIssues.message: "data_admin@company.com" looks like a Kerberos principal — needs mapping to Databricks service principal or managed identity
kerberosIssues.recommendation: Map to a Databricks user via SCIM/IdP integration. Remove the Kerberos realm suffix.
